nodes.name: Opening Lines
nodes.description: Famous first lines of novels and their books

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Call me Ishmael"?
items.explanation:
Herman Melville, published 1851; Ishmael is the sole survivor of the voyage and the novel's narrator
Three words that establish the narrator's ambiguity — we never learn if 'Ishmael' is actually his name
Melville based the story partly on the real sinking of the whaleship Essex (1820) by a sperm whale
The novel flopped on publication and was out of print when Melville died in 1891; rediscovered in the 1920s 'Melville Revival'
Dedicated to Nathaniel Hawthorne, Melville's friend and neighbor in the Berkshires
Ranked among the greatest American novels by Modern Library, Time, and countless critics

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times"?
items.explanation:
Charles Dickens, published 1859; set in London and Paris during the French Revolution
The opening paragraph's six paired oppositions ('the age of wisdom / foolishness,' 'epoch of belief / incredulity') run for over 100 words
The two cities are London and Paris; the tale contrasts English stability with revolutionary French violence
Ends with Sydney Carton's famous sacrifice: 'It is a far, far better thing that I do, than I have ever done'
The best-selling novel of all time by some estimates; has never been out of print
Dickens researched the Revolution using Thomas Carlyle's *The French Revolution*; Carlyle reportedly lent him his entire library
items.cardFront: "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times"
items.cardBack: *A Tale of Two Cities* — Charles Dickens

links.text: "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times"

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen"?
items.explanation:
George Orwell, published 1949; the 'thirteen' signals instantly that this is a society where reality has been altered
Written on the Scottish island of Jura while Orwell was dying of tuberculosis; he died in January 1950
Introduced 'Big Brother,' 'doublethink,' 'Newspeak,' 'thoughtcrime,' and 'Room 101' into English
The title was arrived at by inverting the last two digits of the year Orwell finished it: 1948
Sales spike with every government surveillance scandal; topped bestseller lists after the Snowden leaks (2013)
Orwell's real name was Eric Arthur Blair
items.cardFront: "It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen."

links.text: "It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "All happy families are alike; each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way"?
items.explanation:
Leo Tolstoy, published 1878; the opening applies to both plot strands — Anna's catastrophic affair and Levin's hopeful domestic life
The Russian opening ('Vse schastlivye sem'i pokhozhi drug na druga') is equally famous in Russian literature
Anna's tragedy is foreshadowed from the first pages: a man is killed by a train before Anna and Vronsky even meet
The parallel Levin plot — a landowner seeking meaning through farming and faith — ends with contentment rather than tragedy
Tolstoy reportedly felt Anna 'ran away' from his original intentions, taking the novel in directions he didn't plan
'Vengeance is mine; I will repay' (Romans 12:19) is the epigraph — whose vengeance the novel leaves ambiguous
items.cardFront: "All happy families are alike; each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way."

links.text: "All happy families are alike; each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife"?
items.explanation:
Jane Austen, published 1813; the opening line is ironic — it describes the neighborhood's assumptions, not universal truth
Originally titled 'First Impressions' when drafted in 1796–1797; rejected by publisher Thomas Cadell sight unseen
Austen published all six novels anonymously, credited to 'A Lady'
Follows the five Bennet daughters; Elizabeth and Darcy's enemies-to-lovers arc is the prototypical Regency romance
Colin Firth's 1995 BBC portrayal of Darcy, including the famous wet-shirt scene, created a cultural phenomenon
Austen's face appeared on the British ten-pound note from 2017; she died at 41 in 1817
items.cardFront: "It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife."

links.text: "It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins"?
items.explanation:
Vladimir Nabokov, published 1955; the opening line's alliteration is a key to Humbert's seductive, manipulative prose style
Nabokov wrote it in English (not his first or second language) — one of the most celebrated feats of literary style in any language
Rejected by four American publishers as obscene; first published in Paris by Olympia Press, which specialized in erotica
The name 'Lolita' has entered English as a word for a sexually precocious girl — a use Nabokov rejected as a misreading
Humbert confesses to murdering Quilty in the opening paragraphs — the plot is framed as a legal document
Nabokov also wrote *Pale Fire* (1962) and *Ada* (1969); he was an expert entomologist and classified several butterfly species
items.cardFront: "Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins."

links.text: "Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Someone must have slandered Josef K., for one morning, without having done anything wrong, he was arrested"?
items.explanation:
Franz Kafka, written c. 1914–1915, published posthumously 1925; Kafka instructed Max Brod to burn the manuscript — Brod refused
Josef K. is arrested but never told the charge — the crime is never named and the court never explained
'Kafkaesque' (describing oppressive, senseless bureaucracy) derives from this novel and *The Metamorphosis*
Orson Welles adapted it as a film in 1962 with Anthony Perkins as K.; Welles called it his best film
Kafka wrote in German; he worked as an insurance clerk in Prague for most of his adult life
Died of tuberculosis in 1924 at age 40; his three unfinished novels (*The Trial*, *The Castle*, *Amerika*) were all published after his death
items.cardFront: "Someone must have slandered Josef K., for one morning, without having done anything wrong, he was arrested."

links.text: "Someone must have slandered Josef K., for one morning, without having done anything wrong, he was arrested."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer"?
items.explanation:
Mark Twain, published 1884–1885; the opening immediately establishes Huck's vernacular voice and self-awareness as a character
Huck's reference to Twain's previous novel (*Tom Sawyer*, 1876) is one of literature's earliest meta-fictional nods
Hemingway declared: 'All modern American literature comes from one book by Mark Twain called *Huckleberry Finn*'
Huck's decision to 'go to hell' rather than betray Jim is one of American literature's pivotal moral moments
Famously challenged in libraries almost immediately after publication; still one of the most frequently banned American novels
Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens; 'Mark Twain' was a Mississippi River depth-sounding call (two fathoms, safe water)
items.cardFront: "You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer"

links.text: "You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer"

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice"?
items.explanation:
Gabriel García Márquez, published 1967 in Spanish; the opening plants three time frames simultaneously — a firing squad (future), discovery of ice (past), and an unnamed 'now'
The technique of beginning with a future event and flashing back became García Márquez's signature
The defining novel of magical realism — the fantastical is presented as matter-of-factly as any other event
Has sold over 50 million copies in 46 languages; García Márquez won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982
García Márquez sold his car to support his family while writing it; the advance from his publisher paid off the resulting debt
A Netflix adaptation was released in 2024, the first authorized screen version after decades of García Márquez blocking it
items.cardFront: "Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice."
items.cardBack: *One Hundred Years of Solitude* — Gabriel García Márquez

links.text: "Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "It was a dark and stormy night" — now notorious as the archetypal bad opening line?
items.explanation:
Edward Bulwer-Lytton, published 1830; the full opening sentence runs on for nearly 60 words with multiple semicolons
Bulwer-Lytton was a popular Victorian novelist; 'Paul Clifford' was well-received at the time despite the opening
'The pen is mightier than the sword' (from his 1839 play *Richelieu*) and 'the great unwashed' are both Bulwer-Lytton coinages
The annual Bulwer-Lytton Fiction Contest (est. 1982, San Jose State University) awards the worst possible opening sentence to a novel
Charles Schulz immortalized the line by having Snoopy type it repeatedly as the beginning of his unfinished novel in *Peanuts*
Bulwer-Lytton also coined the phrase 'pursuit of the almighty dollar'
items.cardFront: "It was a dark and stormy night"

links.text: "It was a dark and stormy night"

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Stately, plump Buck Mulligan came from the stairhead"?
items.explanation:
James Joyce, published 1922; set entirely on June 16, 1904 in Dublin — now celebrated annually as 'Bloomsday'
Buck Mulligan (based on real-life Oliver St. John Gogarty) provides the opening scene before Leopold Bloom appears in Chapter 4
Banned in the U.S. for obscenity until the landmark 1933 *United States v. One Book Called Ulysses* ruling
Ranked #1 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels of the 20th Century
Joyce's stream-of-consciousness style — interior monologue following a character's unfiltered thoughts — revolutionized the novel form
The novel's final chapter (Molly Bloom's monologue) runs 46 pages as a single sentence with no punctuation except two final periods
items.cardFront: "Stately, plump Buck Mulligan came from the stairhead"

links.text: "Stately, plump Buck Mulligan came from the stairhead"

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again"?
items.explanation:
Daphne du Maurier, published 1938; Manderley is the fictional Cornish estate haunted by the memory of the first Mrs. de Winter
The unnamed narrator-protagonist is one of literature's rare major characters without a given name
'Rebecca' never appears as a living character — she exerts power from beyond her death throughout the entire novel
Alfred Hitchcock's 1940 film adaptation won the Academy Award for Best Picture — Hitchcock's only Best Picture winner
The novel sold 3 million copies in the U.S. alone during WWII, one of the great wartime bestsellers
Du Maurier based Manderley on Menabilly, a Cornish house she rented from 1943 onward; the novel was written before she lived there
items.cardFront: "Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again."

links.text: "Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "I am an invisible man"?
items.explanation:
Ralph Ellison, published 1952; the narrator's invisibility is metaphorical — white America refuses to see him as an individual
Won the National Book Award in 1953 — Ellison was the first African American to receive it
Ellison began the novel in a barn in Vermont in 1945 while on leave from the Merchant Marine
Ellison's only published novel in his lifetime; he worked on a second for 40+ years without completing it
Not to be confused with H.G. Wells's *The Invisible Man* (1897), which concerns literal physical invisibility
The novel's final line — 'Who knows but that, on the lower frequencies, I speak for you?' — is one of the most celebrated in American fiction

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin"?
items.explanation:
Franz Kafka, published 1915; the transformation is stated as flat fact in the opening clause — no buildup or explanation
Kafka's German 'ungeheueres Ungeziefer' means approximately 'monstrous vermin' — he deliberately refused to specify the creature
Kafka explicitly forbade any illustration of an insect on the cover of the first edition
Gregor's family initially cares for him, then treats him with growing disgust until his death brings them relief
Kafka died of tuberculosis in 1924; this novella and *The Trial* are the works most central to his reputation
The novella is Kafka's own experience of alienation and family burden — he worked a hated office job while writing fiction at night
items.cardFront: "As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin."

links.text: "As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin."

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the line "The sky above the port was the color of television, tuned to a dead channel"?
items.explanation:
William Gibson, published 1984; the opening line defined the aesthetic of cyberpunk — gritty, technological, urban
The first novel to win the Hugo Award, the Nebula Award, and the Philip K. Dick Award — the triple crown of science fiction
Coined the term 'cyberspace': 'A consensual hallucination experienced daily by billions of legitimate operators'
Gibson wrote the novel on a manual typewriter, deliberately avoiding computers, which he found intimidating
Set in a sprawling future megalopolis called 'the Sprawl'; protagonist Case is a washed-up data thief hired for one last job
The novel's visual aesthetic directly influenced *The Matrix* (1999) and most subsequent cyberpunk cinema
items.cardFront: "The sky above the port was the color of television, tuned to a dead channel."

links.text: "The sky above the port was the color of television, tuned to a dead channel."
